## Task: Prepare a Clean Upstream Submission by Splitting and Squashing Commits You are a contributor working on the `mylib` project located at `/home/user/mylib`. You have been developing a feature locally and your history contains a commit with description **"add config parser and update tests"** that mixes two unrelated concerns into a single change: 1. Changes to `src/config.py` — a new configuration parser module 2. Changes to `tests/test_config.py` — unit tests for that parser 3. Changes to `src/utils.py` — a small unrelated utility fix that was accidentally bundled in Before submitting upstream, your maintainer has asked you to produce a clean, logically separated history with exactly three commits (in order from oldest to newest ancestor): * **Commit A**: Description exactly `"add utility fix"` — contains only the changes to `src/utils.py` * **Commit B**: Description exactly `"add config parser"` — contains only the changes to `src/config.py` * **Commit C**: Description exactly `"add config tests"` — contains only the changes to `tests/test_config.py` The working copy (`@`) should be an empty change on top of Commit C with no description. ### Starting State The repository at `/home/user/mylib` is a jj-backed git repository. It begins with the following history (newest first): 1. A working-copy commit `@` (empty, no description) — child of the commit below 2. A commit with description **"add config parser and update tests"** that introduces: * `src/utils.py` with content: ``` def greet(name): return f"Hello, {name}!" ``` * `src/config.py` with content: ``` def parse_config(path): return {"path": path} ``` * `tests/test_config.py` with content: ``` def test_parse_config(): assert parse_config("a") == {"path": "a"} ``` 3. An initial commit with description **"initial commit"** that sets up the repository structure (contains a `README.md` with the content `# mylib\n`) ### What You Must Do You need to rewrite the history so that the mixed commit **"add config parser and update tests"** is replaced by the three clean commits described above (Commit A, Commit B, Commit C — in that ancestor order, with Commit A being the oldest and Commit C the newest non-working-copy commit). Specifically: 1. **Split** the mixed commit `"add config parser and update tests"` into two separate commits. The first split should isolate `src/utils.py` with the description `"add utility fix"`, while the second split keeps `src/config.py` and `tests/test_config.py` together temporarily. 2. **Split** again (or further separate) so that `src/config.py` and `tests/test_config.py` each end up in their own dedicated commits with descriptions `"add config parser"` and `"add config tests"` respectively. Alternatively, you may accomplish the split into three commits through any valid sequence of `jj split`, `jj squash`, `jj describe`, and/or `jj new` operations — the maintainer only cares about the final state of the history, not the exact sequence of operations used. 3. Ensure the final **working copy** (`@`) is an empty commit with no description, parented directly on top of the `"add config tests"` commit. 4. A bookmark named **`feature`** must point to the commit with description `"add config tests"` (Commit C) at the end. ### Verification Conditions The automated test suite will verify: * The repository at `/home/user/mylib` is a valid jj repository. * The linear ancestry from `@` (working copy) through the history contains exactly the following commit descriptions in order (newest to oldest, excluding the working-copy empty change): `"add config tests"`, `"add config parser"`, `"add utility fix"`, `"initial commit"`. * The commit with description `"add utility fix"` modifies exactly one file: `src/utils.py`, and that file contains the `greet` function. * The commit with description `"add config parser"` modifies exactly one file: `src/config.py`, and that file contains the `parse_config` function. * The commit with description `"add config tests"` modifies exactly one file: `tests/test_config.py`, and that file contains the `test_parse_config` function. * The working copy (`@`) is empty (no changed files). * A bookmark named `feature` points to the commit with description `"add config tests"`. ### Notes * Work exclusively inside `/home/user/mylib`. * Do not use `sudo` or root privileges. * Do not modify the `README.md` in the `"initial commit"` — it must remain unchanged. * The jj user identity is already configured in the repository.

Compare file contents between two revisions\r\n  diffedit          Touch up the content changes in a revision with a diff editor\r\n  duplicate         Create new changes with the same content as     File operations\r\n  fix               Update files with formatting fixes or other changes\r\n  gerrit            Interact with Gerrit Code Review\r\n  git               Commands for working with log               Show revision history\r\n  metaedit          Modify the metadata of a revision without changing its content\r\n  new               Create a new, empty change and (by default) edit it <REPOSITORY>\r\n          Path to repository to operate on\r\n          \r\n          By default, Jujutsu searches for the closest .jj/ directory in an ancestor of the current\r\n          working directory.\r\n\r\n      --ignore-working-copy\r\n          Don't snapshot the working copy, and don't update it\r\n          \r\n          By default, Jujutsu snapshots the working copy at the beginning of every command. The\r\n          working copy is also updated at the end of the command, if the command modified the\r\n          working-copy commit (`@`). If you want to avoid snapshotting the working copy and instead\r\n          see a possibly stale working-copy commit, you can use `--ignore-working-copy`. This may be\r\n          useful e.g. in a command prompt, especially if you have another process that commits the\r\n          working copy.\r\n          \r\n          Loading the repository at a specific operation with `--at-operation` implies\r\n          `--ignore-working-copy`.\r\n\r\n      --ignore-immutable\r\n          Allow rewriting immutable commits\r\n          \r\n          By default, Jujutsu prevents rewriting commits in the configured set of immutable commits.\r\n          This option disables that check and lets you rewrite any commit but the root commit.\r\n          \r\n          This option only affects the check. It does not affect the `immutable_heads()` revset or\r\n          the `immutable` template keyword.\r\n\r\n      --at-operation <AT_OPERATION>\r\n          Operation to load the repo at\r\n          \r\n          Operation to load the repo at. By default, Jujutsu loads the repo at the most recent\r\n          operation, or at the merge of the divergent operations if any.\r\n          \r\n          You can use `--at-op=<operation ID>` to see what the repo looked like at an earlier\r\n          operation. For example `jj --at-op=<operation ID> st` will show you what `jj st` would\r\n          have shown you when the given operation had just finished. `--at-op=@` is pretty much the\r\n          same as the default except that divergent operations will never be merged.\r\n          \r\n          Use `jj op log` to find the operation ID you want. Any unambiguous prefix of the operation\r\n          ID is enough.\r\n          \r\n          When loading the repo at an earlier operation, the working copy will be ignored, as if\r\n          `--ignore-working-copy` had been specified.\r\n          \r\n          It is possible to run mutating commands when loading the repo at an earlier operation.\r\n          Doing that is equivalent to having run concurrent commands starting at the earlier\r\n          operation. There's rarely a reason to do that, but it is possible.\r\n          \r\n          [aliases: --at-op]\r\n\r\n      --debug\r\n          Enable debug logging\r\n\r\n      --color <WHEN>\r\n          When to colorize output\r\n          \r\n          [possible values: always, never, debug, auto]\r\n\r\n      --quiet\r\n          Silence non-primary command output\r\n          \r\n          For example, `jj file list` will still list files, but it won't tell you if the working\r\n          copy was snapshotted or if descendants were rebased.\r\n          \r\n          Warnings and errors will still be printed.\r\n\r\n      --no-pager\r\n          Disable the pager\r\n\r\n      --config <NAME=VALUE>\r\n          Additional configuration options (can be repeated)\r\n          \r\n          The name should be specified as TOML dotted keys.
